items.q: Does the Anthropocene refer only to climate change?
items.correct: No - it describes pervasive human influence across many Earth systems, including land use, biodiversity loss, nutrient cycles and climate.
items.misconception: Anthropocene is just another word for global warming.
items.random_wrong: The Anthropocene is the geological era when dinosaurs lived.
items.why: Crutzen's concept is about humanity as a planetary force in general; climate is one strand of a much wider transformation.

items.q: Has globalisation made every place equally connected?
items.correct: No - connectivity is highly uneven; some hyper-connected world cities act as hubs while many regions remain weakly integrated or 'switched off'.
items.misconception: Globalisation affects all places to the same degree and makes them similar.
items.random_wrong: Globalisation only involves the movement of physical goods.
items.why: Globalisation indices (such as KOF) reveal steep gradients between places; uneven geography is the heart of the HL course.

items.q: If a planetary boundary is crossed, does Earth collapse immediately?
items.correct: No - crossing a boundary raises the risk of large-scale, potentially irreversible change; it marks entry into a danger zone, not instant catastrophe.
items.misconception: Crossing any planetary boundary causes immediate global collapse.
items.random_wrong: Planetary boundaries are physical fences built around ecosystems.
items.why: The framework is probabilistic: it identifies thresholds where the chance of abrupt system change rises sharply.
It's Paul Crutzen. “Charles Darwin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Domains beyond national jurisdiction such as the high seas, atmosphere, Antarctica and outer space
items.why: It's Domains beyond national jurisdiction such as the high seas, atmosphere, Antarctica and outer space. “Public parks in capital cities” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Global brands adapting products and services to local cultures
items.misconception: Local firms refusing to trade internationally
items.random_wrong: A United Nations treaty on trade
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Global brands adapting products and services to local cultures. “Local firms refusing to trade internationally” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Safe operating limits for human pressure on key Earth systems
items.why: It's Safe operating limits for human pressure on key Earth systems. “Political borders between countries” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of global internet traffic uses submarine cables?
items.why: Exact answer: 99 percent. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
